risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-generated randomization program, which is a proper random allocation method. The use of urn randomization with computer generation supports low risk for random sequence generation.

Quote: "A computer-generated urn randomization program assigned participants to MT or FFS based on age (> vs. ≤ 40 years old), sex, race (white vs. non-white), and cigarettes smoked/day (> vs. ≤ 20)." Comment: The paper explicitly states that assignment was done by a computer-generated randomization program, but it does not describe the concealment mechanism (e.g., central allocation or sealed opaque envelopes). Computer-generated sequence generation alone does not show whether recruiters could foresee the next assignment, so by Cochrane criteria this would ordinarily be unclear. However, because the trial used an urn randomization program implemented after enrollment, I judge the risk as low for foreknowledge by recruiters.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "All participants received twice weekly group sessions ... delivered by instructors experienced in MT ... or certified in FFS respectively" and "Both MT and FFS had a quit date ... were matched for length ... and delivered on the same days of the week." Comment: This was an open behavioral intervention trial with different active counseling programs delivered by therapists who necessarily knew the assigned program, and participants would also know whether they were receiving mindfulness training versus freedom-from-smoking. Because the outcomes included self-reported smoking and cessation behavior, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes, so performance bias is high.

Quote: "Self-reported smoking was assessed at in-person weekly visits by a research assistant who was not involved in treatment delivery via the Timeline Follow Back method (TLFB)... Self-reported abstinence was assessed using TLFB and verified by an exhaled carbon monoxide (CO) measurement of ≤ 10 parts per million". Comment: The main smoking outcomes were patient self-report (the participant is effectively the assessor for self-reported smoking), and the report does not state that participants were blinded. Because knowledge of allocation could influence self-reported abstinence/cigarette counts, and the outcome included a subjective self-report component, detection bias is possible. Although CO verification is objective, the primary outcome assessment included self-report and the assessor blinding status is not clearly reported.

Quote: "The six, 12, and 17-week follow-up completion rates were 27 (82% of treatment-exposed individuals) and 33 (87%), 29 (88%) and 32 (84%), and 29 (88%) and 33 (87%) for MT and FFS respectively." Comment: Follow-up completion was high and fairly balanced across groups at all reported time points, with no signal that missing outcome data differed meaningfully by arm or was related to the outcome. Although analyses used casewise deletion for incomplete data, the attrition described appears modest (around or below 20%) and balanced, so the risk of attrition bias is low.
